item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, thanks so much for this comment, it legitimately gave me a lot to think about. I think that, actually, you're right. In retrospect, just having them be called "Princess" would have been just as effective and not involved using an odd symbol.

The dysphoria metaphor, I get being weird - ultimately it's probably weird because my personal dysphoria didn't end up really being… anything? When I wrote this, I was questioning my own gender and after about a month I came to the conclusion that I was in fact cis. That's not supposed to excuse lackluster writing, and I think your idea of it has an interesting meaning as well, but that's why it's a bit odd - for myself, that specific metaphor more about body image than necessarily dysphoria.

The final log is very much meant to feel… I wouldn't say rushed, but very fast and leaving you feeling confused. I'm quite willing to accept that some people don't enjoy that sort of ending, it's very understandable, but for what I want it to be, it is what it is. (also, yes, the words in different font are that way for a reason)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i have a lot of thoughts about this, so sorry this is long

ill start with the things i did like about this! i will say i really like the idea of a skip where the anomaly is just… their subconscious? (or some such) telling them that theyre gay/trans through the anomaly. its a trope i like a lot! i like that the objects manifested are not just related to being gay/trans, but also just… stuff! (of course, you can make a case against the comics and soda just being stuff, and interpret it to be a metaphor) i also like the dialogue between 𝒫 and dr ozid. i love skips where the researchers treat what is, literally or essentially, a human as such.

however, i am finding this execution lacking. the huge head dysphoria metaphor is… not doing it for me. i understand the intent, but i feel it would be more effective 𝒫 sees and feels the change, but everyone else either didnt, or didnt say anything. (perhaps through antimemetic properties? they can all see it, but the antimeme prevents them from saying it out loud? or maybe they just straight up cant see it, which is more like dysphoria)

also, what is the point of having the skip called 𝒫, and not just "Princess"? its not like you cant do that— just look at allison eckhart. i know 𝒫 looks cooler, but its going to make this skip very hard to search, or talk about anywhere but this page.

the final long seems… rushed? or like theres something missing? i do understand that its supposed to be a metaphor, but it just ends up being confusing. is the font change supposed to mean anything, or is it just visual flair? is something thats happening being replaced by the anomaly? is this completely literal? i sure dont know! maybe thats the point, but its falling flat for me.

tl;dr: interesting concept, execution does nothing for me. no vote, but its so close to being a +1!

edit: changing the phrasing of 2 sentences

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that writing dialogue is hard and all, but I still don't think it's up to par.

-1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thirded. that was quite a shift at the end with no real context, and no satisfactory solution. i get the theme you were going for overall, but the execution is lacking. i too have to -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The buildup is very interesting and unique (perfect for the domc), and the story being told here is quite interesting. Plus, that final video log is… whew.

+1

𝒫 reached a rating of 100+.

𝒫 has a little party.

𝒫 has a little party.

𝒫 has a little party.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In case you're wondering, here's what the character looks like

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for technical reasons. I have three devices that I access the wiki with, and none of them can render the character that makes up the title of this. I'm honestly sick of the readability issues caused by people deciding to put emojis or whatever into their articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're not wrong, though in this case it was a conscious decision for the message of the piece. I think a lot of authors, certainly including myself, feel comfortable giving researchers the option to treat human scps like actual humans, since it (probably) isn't actively making containment easier to be inhumane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not using gendered pronouns for humanoid SCPs is actually pretty standard.

Shows how detached the Foundation can be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The beginning and middle parts of this are good, and I really like the characterization of the main character as a bewildered teenager. But I have to agree with others that the ending, especially the final log, is just too random. This feels like it’s building toward a profound statement on identity, but it doesn’t really get there. That said, it started well and is well-written. I give it an no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

I enjoyed the first part of this, and probably would have upvoted1 were it not for the inclusion of the final log. Other commenters have echoed my concerns already, but I feel like the article generally has a lot of interesting things happening that aren't connected too well - it feels like no single sub-anomaly is tied to another. Execution-wise, I appreciated the realism of the dialogue.

I will say that I don't understand why the second page of comments has picked up a theme of dysphoria.

Footnotes
1. I changed my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ball of a head is a metaphor for body dysmorphia or gender dysphoria.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is so utterly bizzare and incongruous and let somehow perfectly cohesive and memorable. Great job, maybe unintentional but this scp really captures dysphoria well :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand what the swollen multi-orificed nose ball for a head signifies.

The article states at one point that the eyes are non-functional yet the skip is described as "seeing" things. Is it blind or sighted? I am confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, I really love the idea and it really got me into the mystery of it quickly.

At the end, I had a lot of questions, but I felt like some of them should have been documented by the Foundation here.

What do the unusual fonts in Addendum 4 indicate? It's text presumably transcribed from a video log. Are they indicating memetic issues in recording, viewing, or transcribing the video?

What were the guests? I couldn't spot any foreshadowing in the patterns of objects received or any of her interviews, but I may just be missing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with your bit about 𝒫 being memetic as a word. The story seems mostly about character development, which is fine, but this doesn't play into that. It even adds the necessary audio. Also, does "𝒫 (formerly Nudah Borvitch) is an 18-year-old human…" seem to contradict that? I just kinda expect a big payoff if a skip's using non-standard formatting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who's currently questioning their gender, this scip really resonates with me. The LGBT metaphor wasn't lost on me before the final addendum, but it seemed a little too comedic. But damn, that last scene is incredible. I was genuinely horrified when it said they "had a little party" for 16 minutes; it's so unsettling and creepy. That might not have been the intent, but I think it perfectly captures the feeling of "wrongness" that comes with dysphoria. To me, the obvious ending would be that the main character starts to embrace their queerness, but having them still reject that side of themselves is genius — it isn't as simple as "I guess I'm a girl now", and it's completely valid to still reject that side of you, because self identity isn't clear-cut. +1.

Edit: I also wanted to say that the choice to not use gendered pronouns for 𝒫 is a great touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved reading this, and the section at the end definitely helps with fully understanding what is happening to the skip. It wasn't too bland or too complex, so I'm going to give this a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this has too many moving parts. The different effects felt excessively random, as others have said, and the themes were only touched on superficially. I do quite appreciate what you were going for here, with a piece of writing about sexuality, identity, appearance and self-image perhaps being thematically appropriate to be confused and messy, but I don't think it really works well in the SCP format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice skip overall. I'm gonna have to stick with novote on this one tho.

Will start with the downsides first: I can't help but feel that there's some disconnect between the original theme of the skip and the one that appears at the last addendum. Not that the effect is too weird, but the whole thing with altered text in the log and all that classy, royal feel of the objects kinda clash with regular anomalous person vibe that was presented throughout the SCP. I don't know whether it's an intentional effect, but it does feel like it comes out of the blue there. Secondly, I'm not sure if I missed something, but it seems like 𝒫 which is the memetic word is kinda insignificant for the story, and I feel like it doesn't affect anything that much.

I feel like those two problems are kinda connected to each other and together create a feel that the skip's story is abruptly cut by the last log, rather than being finished in a natural way.

There are more upsides to this, however: The anomalous effect is nice, albeit it's a bit unclear what we're dealing with from the initial description, and the character was well-written enough for me to be engaged in what's going to happen to him. I've seen quite a few attempts at writing humanoids at allusions for IRL problems, but they rarely come with an interesting enough anomaly to feel like their own thing. This one managed to do that, so I gotta commend the writing here. The pacing in the main part is decent too, it manages to cover everything without dragging for too long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good point, and though to a certain degree I'd like to say the abruptness is intentional, I would agree that if there's one thing I could improve significantly on this, it's the pacing. I wanted to make it feel like everything was sort of falling into 𝒫's lap before they had any time to react, and to reflect that somewhat in the style, but I'm not sure I was able to find the healthiest balance for that. I think I'll give it a week, then return to the piece and see if anything new stands out to me as a good idea for making the pacing more clearly reflect the ending I'm going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, agreed - the randomness and assorted effects, especially towards the end, didn't feel cohesive or building towards a comprehensive enough tone or theme or aesthetic, so it came off as weirdness for weirdness' sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This felt like it was building somewhere, but the end section came so abruptly with so many new inclusions compared to everything that happened before that it threw off any pacing there was.

-1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This rocks. A perfectly haunting SCP with a well-documented psychological fallout, and an opinion-piece on self-image and sexuality. It's awesome, in a way not many SCPs I've read have been.

Take my damn +1 and go be neat someplace else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't like the inclusion of Rouslin's antennae, but I appreciate what they add to the article nonetheless.

To me, the article felt random yet constrained, and given the themes you were aiming for I think you pulled that off well, so +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 𝒫 and a stuffed bear

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, yeah thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure how I feel about this one. Tentative upvote, I think?

EDIT: SPaG thing I pointed out was fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is profoundly weird and uncomfortable and I don't really know what's going on fully but I think I like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bouta have a little party with your fuckin’ MOM lmao gottem.

+1, it good and weird and unnerving and those are all welcome things

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
June Edit: Happy Pride Month!

So, I'm finally back! Hooray! This is a draft I've been working on for months, and I'm not sure how well received it will be, but it means a lot to me personally in terms of its core ideas.

Spoilers, possibly
Or maybe not what do I know

This piece is really supposed to be a coming of age sort of thing, and I wrote it at a time where I was thinking a lot about my own self image, with regards to things like sexuality, gender, and more broadly, my place in life. I don't think I need to get super deep, but a lot of those insecurities are what I used as inspiration for this piece, and I really hope it comes across, and that it's appreciable.

I got a ton of critters over the course of the last 3 months, and I stupidly only kept track of a few of them, but I absolutely appreciate everyone who looked over this draft. Thank you.

If anyone is re-reading this and is confused about the disappearance of Dr. Rouslin, I'm renaming him to Dr. Ozid in all my articles. I felt like it was a little weird to have my author insert share my full name, so that's what that is.
